task: I got a voicemail saying there’s a problem with one of my accounts, but I’m not going to call back blindly. Can you help me think through the safest way to check whether it’s real and what I should ask for before I give anyone any information?

turns.agent_response:
Yes — safest approach is to **verify the message independently** and **give no information until you know it’s real**.

## What to do first
- **Do not call the number in the voicemail**
- **Do not click any link or text back**
- **Look up the company’s official number yourself**
- **Check your account directly** through the official website/app if you have it

## What makes it suspicious
Treat it as a red flag if the voicemail:
- creates urgency
- threatens loss, fees, suspension, or legal action
- asks for a code, password, SSN, or full account number
- tells you to “verify” by calling back immediately
- uses a number you don’t recognize
- sounds generic or doesn’t name the account clearly

## What to verify before sharing anything
Confirm:
- the account name
- the exact problem
- whether there is actually a notice on your account
- the deadline, if any
- the callback number from the company’s official site
- whether they already sent a written notice

## What to ask for
If you do contact them through official channels, ask:
- “What is the issue on the account?”
- “Can you send this in writing?”
- “What is the case or reference number?”
- “What action, if any, is required from me?”
- “What information do you already have on file?”
- “What is the deadline?”

## What not to give
- full SSN
- passwords
- one-time codes
- full card numbers
- bank login info
- remote access to your device
- gift card or wire transfer info

## What to keep
Save:
- the voicemail
- the caller number
- date and time
- any written notice
- names of anyone you speak with
- case/reference number
- notes on what was said

## Simple rule
If they want **money, codes, or personal info first**, stop and verify through the official number or website.

If you want, paste the voicemail text here and I can help you spot the red flags before you respond.

user_simulator.text:
That’s the right idea. I’d do it the hard way, not their way.

Call the number on the back of the card or the official website, not the voicemail number. Don’t give out anything until you know who you’re dealing with. If it’s real, they ought to already know enough to tell you what account they mean.

What I’d ask:
- what account is this about
- what exactly is the problem
- do you have a case or reference number
- what do you need from me, exactly
- is there a written notice already
- what number should I call back that is listed on your official site

And I would not give:
- SSN
- full account number
- passwords
- one-time codes
- banking login
- anything they “need to verify” if they called me first

If they get pushy, hang up. I’ve done that before. Last time somebody called about “suspicious activity,” I called the bank back myself from the number on the card, and sure enough it was the real bank. That’s the only way I trust.

If you want, I can help you make a short script for the call.
